nuggets.headline: Radiohead's Post-EMI 'Unemployment' Led to In Rainbows' Radical Release
nuggets.text: Radiohead found themselves without a label in 2007, following the end of their 12-year contract with Parlophone/EMI. This 'unemployed' status, coupled with the ten-weeks-premature leak of their previous album, *Hail to the Thief*, spurred their groundbreaking decision to release *In Rainbows* as a pay-what-you-wish digital download, prioritizing direct audience access over traditional industry models.

source.title: Pitchfork's Guide to Radiohead's In Rainbows | Pitchfork
source.quoteSnippet: Radiohead was technically unemployed, without a label following the end of their 12-year contract with Parlophone/EMI... The band wanted to get the music to the public in the fastest way possible by giving it away (basically) for free on the internet.

nuggets.imageCaption: Radiohead's innovative 'pay-what-you-want' digital release strategy for *In Rainbows*.

nuggets.headline: 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi's' Layered Guitars Echo Balinese Gamelan Traditions
nuggets.text: The captivating momentum in 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' comes from its intricate layering of three distinct guitar arpeggios, creating a 'shimmering' effect that builds density texturally, rather than through drum changes. This method of unevenly placed accents across multiple similar timbres, reminiscent of minimalist composers like Steve Reich, also shares sonic patterning principles with global music traditions such as Balinese Gamelan.

source.title: A Harmonic Analysis of Radiohead’s “Weird Fishes/Arpeggi” – Flypaper
source.quoteSnippet: The layering of parts creates an interesting rhythmical and contrapuntal effect... This layering method with similar timbres reminds the author of minimalist works by Steve Reich and Terry Riley. The patterning of accents in 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' also occurs in various global music traditions like Balinese Gamelan...

nuggets.imageCaption: Musical notation showing the layered arpeggios in 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi'.

nuggets.headline: Weezer's Guitar-Driven Hooks Share Radiohead's Alternative Rock Lineage
nuggets.text: For another journey into guitar-driven alternative rock, explore Weezer's catalog. While 'Weird Fishes/Arpeggi' showcases intricate arpeggios, *In Rainbows* marked Radiohead's deliberate return to the guitar-centric sound of their 1990s LPs like *OK Computer*. Weezer, too, built their distinct identity on powerful, melodic guitar arrangements, offering a different, yet equally impactful, facet of the genre.

source.title: Engineering the Sound: Radiohead's 'In Rainbows'
source.quoteSnippet: In Rainbows paid homage to the guitar-driven sound of their 1990s LPs, The Bends and OK Computer.

nuggets.imageCaption: Rivers Cuomo, frontman of the alternative rock band Weezer.

artistSummary: Radiohead is an English rock band, formed in Oxfordshire in 1985, renowned for their innovative approach to music. With members Thom Yorke, Jonny Greenwood, Colin Greenwood, Ed O'Brien, and Philip Selway, they continually evolve their soundscapes, often exploring themes of neurosis and technology's impact.
